\Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Responses To Defendants Requests For Production And Accompanying Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Oct-13-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Luke Nikas, Esq. (212.849.7000; lukenikas@quinnemanuel.com) of Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan LLP for Defendant.
No other appearances.

THE PARTIES STIPULATE TO THE MATTER BEING DECIDED BY THE JUDGE PRO TEM. THE PARTIES AND THE JUDGE PRO TEM APPEARING REMOTELY AUTHORIZE THE CLERK TO SIGN THE FORMAL STIPULATION FORM ON THEIR BEHALF.

DISCUSSED. The Court noted the contest to tentative ruling was untimely and there being no appearance by the other side, it cannot hear oral arguments. Court is willing to hear oral argument in special set time if parties agree to have oral arguments.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN BURBANK III's Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Further Responses To Defendant's Requests For Production:

Motion to compel GRANTED in part and DENIED in part as follows. RFP 17 - denied. The response is sufficient. That Cook may have anticipated Schelper would access the home does not prove that there are written communications about that issue. People frequently communicate in ways that does not leave a written record. RFP 21 - denied. The amended response is sufficient. RFP 31 - granted. California law recognizes a taxpayer privilege regarding tax returns. See Schnabel v. Sup. Ct. (1993) 5 Cal.4th 704 (citing Sammut v. Sammut (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 557). However, there are exceptions when there is intentional relinquishment of the right to privacy or waiver of privilege. There is no dispute that Burbank's staff prepared at least first drafts of Cook's tax returns, which supports a finding of relinquishment or waiver as between them. How Cook reported to tax authorities any monies she received from Burbank is directly relevant to the issues in this litigation, including whether there are any inconsistencies with how he reported those monies or whether there were any changes after Burbank's staff completed the drafts. RFP 32 - granted in part. This request seeks documents sufficient to show each instance when Burbank transferred money to Cook during the defined period; the request for locations of all bank accounts is subsidiary to that request. The response indicates that Cook will produce documents sufficient to show such transfers, which is what was requested. To the extent Burbank seeks to compel production based on his subsidiary request by broadening it to include locations of all bank accounts even if not related to transfers from him, it would make the RFP impermissibly compound and impermissibly overbroad. The request is granted in part to the extent that Cook must produce such documents that are sufficient to show transfers from Burbank but not all accounts without limitation or relation to these proceedings. RFP 38 - denied. The response is sufficient and the Court cannot compel Cook to answer a request that assumes a contested factual point, and the current response correctly asserts that objection.

Prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling via email to sborrowman@gmail.com.

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-27-2022 Continued To May-24-22 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-24-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant John Burbank III's demurrer to Plaintiff Jordan Cook's amended complaint is sustained and overruled as follows:

Defendant's demurrer is sustained with leave to amend as to Plaintiff's first cause of action for conversion.

"A cause of action for conversion requires allegations of plaintiff's ownership or right to possession of property; defendant's wrongful act toward or disposition of the property, interfering with plaintiff's possession; and damage to plaintiff." (PCO, Inc. v. Christensen, Miller, Fink, Jacobs, Glaser, Weil & Shapiro, LLP (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 384, 395.)

Plaintiff sufficiently alleges that Defendant wrongfully exercised dominion and control over the property "by converting it to his own use and/or retaining it without any claim of ownership and without Plaintiff's consent." (FAC, 14.) Additionally, Plaintiff describes the property with sufficient specificity. Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's use of "including, but not limited to," could be read to include all the property in Defendant's possession is unpersuasive. That phrase applies to specific categories of property allegedly located in specific locations, i.e. "sculptures, paintings, and other artwork from notable professional artists" (followed by lists of the artists' names and titles of the works) located at the Sea Cliff Property, the Nimble Vineyard property, and in a storage facility. (FAC, 8-10.) Her other descriptions of property ("Furniture and books inherited by Plaintiff from her grandmother and great grandmother"; "Plaintiff's clothes"; "Plaintiff's jewelry"; "A large bowl with leather handles that was a gift to Plaintiff from her mother"; and "A black and white tray that was a gift to Plaintiff from her mother") provide sufficient notice to Defendant.

However, Plaintiff's allegations of ownership or right to possession of the subject property are conclusory. As such, Plaintiff is granted leave to amend to allege facts regarding her ownership or right to possession.

Defendant's demurrer is overruled as to Plaintiff's second cause of action "to recover possession of personal property or its value and for damages for wrongful detention."

Plaintiff's second cause of action seeks specific recovery of personal property and is properly pleaded. (See Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guar. Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38 ["If the complaint states a cause of action under any theory, regardless of the title under which the factual basis for relief is stated, that aspect of the complaint is good against a demurrer."].) Defendant's argument that it was duplicative for Plaintiff to also include damages in her second cause of action is inapposite. (Daniels v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc. (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1150, 1167 ["a general demurrer may not be sustained as to a portion of a cause of action."].)

Counsel for Defendant are admonished to serve courtesy copies of all pleadings for future hearings in Department 302. Failure to comply with Local Rule 2.7(B) may result in motions being removed from the calendar. Order signed.

Judge: Suzanne Bolanos, Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/SRB)

DEFENDANT JOHN BURBANK III'S Motion For Issuance Of Letter Of Request For International Judicial Assistance.

The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted. Defendant's motion for issuance of letter of request for international judicial assistance is granted. The Court views the letters rogatory procedure in California as primarily ministerial and not to require more than a showing that issuance is "necessary and convenient." The procedure doesn't require evidentiary support, nor a detailed analysis. The Court finds that in this case Defendant has made a sufficient showing. That being said, Plaintiff and any third party witnesses preserve all objections to the subpoenas, including relevance, and may raise those objections in the courts of the United Kingdom.

Order is signed.

Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion To Quash Or Modify Subpoenas
Off Calendar Oct-27-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

There being no appearances, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on Plaintiff Jordan Cook's Notice Of Motion To Quash Or Modify Subpoenas as follows:

The motion to quash the subpoena of Alexandra Schelper is denied. She was served at her residence in San Francisco. The parties are directed to meet and confer regarding the scope of the documents requested in the subpoena and the date/location for the deposition. If the parties continue to have disputes on these issues, they are directed to contact the JPT via email to set up a hearing to resolve the matter. The motion to quash the subpoenas of Joe Harvey, Josh Harvey, Glen deLaine, Jeanne Bautier and Nicole Kluk is granted. Defendant acknowledges that the subpoenas do not comply with the Hague Convention Letters Rogatory procedure, but that they sought voluntary compliance to avoid costs. However, it is unlikely that a non-party, unrepresented person in the UK would know the difference between a properly issued subpoena by the UK Courts versus one issued by this Court which has threatening language regarding compliance. Defendant appears to be entitled to testimony from these witnesses, but if they reside outside of California, and the US, then proper Letter Rogatory procedures must be followed absent an agreement with the non-party or his/her counsel. The requests for sanctions are denied.

The Court will review the proposed order submitted by the plaintiff.

Judge Pro Tem: David McDonald, Clerk: J. Alameda, Not reported. =(302/JPT)
